Problem Statement¶

Business Context¶

Business communities in the United States are facing high demand for human resources, but one of the constant challenges is identifying and attracting the right talent, which is perhaps the most important element in remaining competitive. Companies in the United States look for hard-working, talented, and qualified individuals both locally as well as abroad.

The Immigration and Nationality Act (INA) of the US permits foreign workers to come to the United States to work on either a temporary or permanent basis. The act also protects US workers against adverse impacts on their wages or working conditions by ensuring US employers' compliance with statutory requirements when they hire foreign workers to fill workforce shortages. The immigration programs are administered by the Office of Foreign Labor Certification (OFLC).

OFLC processes job certification applications for employers seeking to bring foreign workers into the United States and grants certifications in those cases where employers can demonstrate that there are not sufficient US workers available to perform the work at wages that meet or exceed the wage paid for the occupation in the area of intended employment.

Objective¶

In FY 2016, the OFLC processed 775,979 employer applications for 1,699,957 positions for temporary and permanent labor certifications. This was a nine percent increase in the overall number of processed applications from the previous year. The process of reviewing every case is becoming a tedious task as the number of applicants is increasing every year.

The increasing number of applicants every year calls for a Machine Learning based solution that can help in shortlisting the candidates having higher chances of VISA approval. OFLC has hired the firm EasyVisa for data-driven solutions. You as a data scientist at EasyVisa have to analyze the data provided and, with the help of a classification model:

  • Facilitate the process of visa approvals.
  • Recommend a suitable profile for the applicants for whom the visa should be certified or denied based on the drivers that significantly influence the case status.

Data Description¶

The data contains the different attributes of employee and the employer. The detailed data dictionary is given below.

  • case_id: ID of each visa application
  • continent: Information of continent the employee
  • education_of_employee: Information of education of the employee
  • has_job_experience: Does the employee has any job experience? Y= Yes; N = No
  • requires_job_training: Does the employee require any job training? Y = Yes; N = No
  • no_of_employees: Number of employees in the employer's company
  • yr_of_estab: Year in which the employer's company was established
  • region_of_employment: Information of foreign worker's intended region of employment in the US.
  • prevailing_wage: Average wage paid to similarly employed workers in a specific occupation in the area of intended employment. The purpose of the prevailing wage is to ensure that the foreign worker is not underpaid compared to other workers offering the same or similar service in the same area of employment.
  • unit_of_wage: Unit of prevailing wage. Values include Hourly, Weekly, Monthly, and Yearly.
  • full_time_position: Is the position of work full-time? Y = Full Time Position; N = Part Time Position
  • case_status: Flag indicating if the Visa was certified or denied

In [ ]:
# Libraries to help with reading and manipulating data
import numpy as np
import pandas as pd

# Library to split data
from sklearn.model_selection import train_test_split

# To oversample and undersample data
from imblearn.over_sampling import SMOTE
from imblearn.under_sampling import RandomUnderSampler
from sklearn.model_selection import train_test_split, StratifiedKFold, cross_val_score


# libaries to help with data visualization
import matplotlib.pyplot as plt
import seaborn as sns

# Removes the limit for the number of displayed columns
pd.set_option("display.max_columns", None)
# Sets the limit for the number of displayed rows
pd.set_option("display.max_rows", 100)


# Libraries different ensemble classifiers
from sklearn.ensemble import (
    BaggingClassifier,
    RandomForestClassifier,
    AdaBoostClassifier,
    GradientBoostingClassifier
)

from xgboost import XGBClassifier
from sklearn.tree import DecisionTreeClassifier

# Libraries to get different metric scores
from sklearn import metrics
from sklearn.metrics import (
    confusion_matrix,
    accuracy_score,
    precision_score,
    recall_score,
    f1_score,
)

# To tune different models
from sklearn.model_selection import RandomizedSearchCV

# To ignore unnecessary warnings
import warnings
warnings.filterwarnings("ignore")

Exploratory Data Analysis (EDA)¶

Let's check the statistical summary of the data¶

In [ ]:
data.describe().T
Out[ ]:
count mean std min 25% 50% 75% max
no_of_employees 25480.0 5667.043210 22877.928848 -26.0000 1022.00 2109.00 3504.0000 602069.00
yr_of_estab 25480.0 1979.409929 42.366929 1800.0000 1976.00 1997.00 2005.0000 2016.00
prevailing_wage 25480.0 74455.814592 52815.942327 2.1367 34015.48 70308.21 107735.5125 319210.27
Observations:¶
  • One strange observation about no_of_employees is that the min for no_of_employess is -26, which seems to be an anomaly. This most likely seems to be a typo and can be corrected by replacing it with it's absolute value
  • The min for prevailing_wage is 2.1367 and max for prevailing_wage is 319210.27. These values seem to be outside of the IQR for this dataset, which indicates that there maybe some outliers for this column

Fixing the negative values in number of employees columns¶

# Fixing the negative values in number of employees columns by taking the absolute value

Observations:¶
  • case_status: The target variable is imbalanced, with Certified cases (17018) being significantly more frequent than Denied cases (8462).
  • employer_name: There are a large number of unique employer names, with the top employers having a much higher number of cases than others. This suggests potential employer-specific patterns in visa applications.
  • job_title: Similar to employer names, there's a wide variety of job titles, with some titles appearing much more frequently. This could indicate common roles for visa applications.
  • full_time_position: This variable is heavily skewed towards Y (Yes), indicating that most visa applications are for full-time positions.
  • soc_name: There's a diverse range of SOC names, representing different occupational categories. Some categories are more prevalent than others.
  • work_state: California is the most frequent work state, followed by other populous states. This aligns with expected patterns of immigration and employment.
  • visa_class: The H-1B visa class is overwhelmingly the most common, which is expected given the nature of the dataset.
  • prevailing_wage_unit_of_measure: Year is the most common unit of measure for prevailing wage, which is standard for annual salaries.
  • industry_unit_of_measure: Year is also the most common unit of measure for industry, which is less intuitive and might require further investigation or clarification of what this variable represents.
  • continent: Asia is the most represented continent, which is consistent with global immigration trends to the US.

Univariate Analysis¶

In [ ]:
def histogram_boxplot(data, feature, figsize=(15, 10), kde=False, bins=None):
    """
    Boxplot and histogram combined

    data: dataframe
    feature: dataframe column
    figsize: size of figure (default (15,10))
    kde: whether to show the density curve (default False)
    bins: number of bins for histogram (default None)
    """
    f2, (ax_box2, ax_hist2) = plt.subplots(
        nrows=2,  # Number of rows of the subplot grid= 2
        sharex=True,  # x-axis will be shared among all subplots
        gridspec_kw={"height_ratios": (0.25, 0.75)},
        figsize=figsize,
    )  # creating the 2 subplots
    sns.boxplot(
        data=data, x=feature, ax=ax_box2, showmeans=True, color="violet"
    )  # boxplot will be created and a triangle will indicate the mean value of the column
    sns.histplot(
        data=data, x=feature, kde=kde, ax=ax_hist2, bins=bins
    ) if bins else sns.histplot(
        data=data, x=feature, kde=kde, ax=ax_hist2
    )  # For histogram
    ax_hist2.axvline(
        data[feature].mean(), color="green", linestyle="--"
    )  # Add mean to the histogram
    ax_hist2.axvline(
        data[feature].median(), color="black", linestyle="-"
    )  # Add median to the histogram
In [ ]:
# function to create labeled barplots


def labeled_barplot(data, feature, perc=False, n=None):
    """
    Barplot with percentage at the top

    data: dataframe
    feature: dataframe column
    perc: whether to display percentages instead of count (default is False)
    n: displays the top n category levels (default is None, i.e., display all levels)
    """

    total = len(data[feature])  # length of the column
    count = data[feature].nunique()
    if n is None:
        plt.figure(figsize=(count + 1, 5))
    else:
        plt.figure(figsize=(n + 1, 5))

    plt.xticks(rotation=90, fontsize=15)
    ax = sns.countplot(
        data=data,
        x=feature,
        palette="Paired",
        order=data[feature].value_counts().index[:n].sort_values(),
    )

    for p in ax.patches:
        if perc == True:
            label = "{:.1f}%".format(
                100 * p.get_height() / total
            )  # percentage of each class of the category
        else:
            label = p.get_height()  # count of each level of the category

        x = p.get_x() + p.get_width() / 2  # width of the plot
        y = p.get_height()  # height of the plot

        ax.annotate(
            label,
            (x, y),
            ha="center",
            va="center",
            size=12,
            xytext=(0, 5),
            textcoords="offset points",
        )  # annotate the percentage

    plt.show()  # show the plot

Observations on education of employee¶

In [ ]:
labeled_barplot(data, "education_of_employee", perc=True)
Observations:¶
  • Bachelors and Masters is the preferred education level of employees with 40.2% & 37.8%
  • Doctorate has the lowest percentage count amongst all education levels with only 8.6%

  • Island has the lowest employment with 1.5%

Observations on case status¶

labeled_barplot(data, "case_status", perc=True)
Observations¶
  • 66.8% have their case status as Certified whereas the remaining 33.2% have their case status as Denied

Observation on numerical columns¶

In [ ]:
# Select numerical columns
numerical_cols = data.select_dtypes(include=np.number).columns

# Loop through numerical columns and create plots
for col in numerical_cols:
    histogram_boxplot(data, col)
Observations:¶
  • no_of_employees: The distribution is highly skewed to the right, indicating that most companies have a relatively small number of employees, with a few companies having a very large number of employees. There are also some outliers with extremely high numbers of employees.
  • yr_of_estab: The distribution shows a wide range of establishment years, with a peak around the late 1900s and early 2000s. There are also some older companies established in the 1800s. This column is highly skewed to the left
  • prevailing_wage: The distribution is skewed to the right, suggesting that most prevailing wages are lower, with some outliers having very high wages. This aligns with the initial observation from the statistical summary.

Bivariate Analysis¶

Correlation check¶

In [ ]:
cols_list = data.select_dtypes(include=np.number).columns.tolist()

plt.figure(figsize=(10, 5))
sns.heatmap(
    data[cols_list].corr(), annot=True, vmin=-1, vmax=1, fmt=".2f", cmap="Spectral"
)
plt.show()
Observations:¶
  • The correlation values are close to zero, indicating a very weak linear relationship between these numerical variables. There is no significant correlation observed between any pair of these numerical features.
  • This suggests that these numerical features are largely independent of each other in terms of linear relationships.

Creating functions that will help us with further analysis.¶

In [ ]:
### function to plot distributions wrt target


def distribution_plot_wrt_target(data, predictor, target):

    fig, axs = plt.subplots(2, 2, figsize=(12, 10))

    target_uniq = data[target].unique()

    axs[0, 0].set_title("Distribution of target for target=" + str(target_uniq[0]))
    sns.histplot(
        data=data[data[target] == target_uniq[0]],
        x=predictor,
        kde=True,
        ax=axs[0, 0],
        color="teal",
        stat="density",
    )

    axs[0, 1].set_title("Distribution of target for target=" + str(target_uniq[1]))
    sns.histplot(
        data=data[data[target] == target_uniq[1]],
        x=predictor,
        kde=True,
        ax=axs[0, 1],
        color="orange",
        stat="density",
    )

    axs[1, 0].set_title("Boxplot w.r.t target")
    sns.boxplot(data=data, x=target, y=predictor, ax=axs[1, 0], palette="gist_rainbow")

    axs[1, 1].set_title("Boxplot (without outliers) w.r.t target")
    sns.boxplot(
        data=data,
        x=target,
        y=predictor,
        ax=axs[1, 1],
        showfliers=False,
        palette="gist_rainbow",
    )

    plt.tight_layout()
    plt.show()
In [ ]:
def stacked_barplot(data, predictor, target):
    """
    Print the category counts and plot a stacked bar chart

    data: dataframe
    predictor: independent variable
    target: target variable
    """
    count = data[predictor].nunique()
    sorter = data[target].value_counts().index[-1]
    tab1 = pd.crosstab(data[predictor], data[target], margins=True).sort_values(
        by=sorter, ascending=False
    )
    print(tab1)
    print("-" * 120)
    tab = pd.crosstab(data[predictor], data[target], normalize="index").sort_values(
        by=sorter, ascending=False
    )
    tab.plot(kind="bar", stacked=True, figsize=(count + 5, 5))
    plt.legend(
        loc="lower left", frameon=False,
    )
    plt.legend(loc="upper left", bbox_to_anchor=(1, 1))
    plt.show()

Does higher education increase the chances of visa certification for well-paid jobs abroad?¶

In [ ]:
stacked_barplot(data, "education_of_employee", "case_status")

case_status            Certified  Denied    All
education_of_employee                          
All                        17018    8462  25480
Bachelor's                  6367    3867  10234
High School                 1164    2256   3420
Master's                    7575    2059   9634
Doctorate                   1912     280   2192
------------------------------------------------------------------------------------------------------------------------
Observations:¶
  • Bachelor's and Master's degrees are the most common education levels among the applicants in this dataset, accounting for the largest number of both Certified and Denied cases in absolute terms.
  • Applicants with Master's and Doctorate degrees have a higher proportion of Certified cases compared to those with Bachelor's degrees or High School education. This suggests that higher education levels might be associated with a greater likelihood of visa certification.
  • Applicants with High School education have the lowest proportion of Certified cases and the highest proportion of Denied cases, indicating that a High School diploma alone might not be sufficient for a high chance of visa approval in this dataset.
  • Applicants with a Doctorate degree have the highest certification rate, with a very low number of denied cases. This strongly suggests that a Doctorate is a favorable factor for visa certification.

How does visa status vary across different continents?¶

In [ ]:
stacked_barplot(data, "continent", "case_status")

case_status    Certified  Denied    All
continent                              
All                17018    8462  25480
Asia               11012    5849  16861
North America       2037    1255   3292
Europe              2957     775   3732
South America        493     359    852
Africa               397     154    551
Oceania              122      70    192
------------------------------------------------------------------------------------------------------------------------
Observations:¶
  • Asia has the highest number of total applications, as well as the highest number of certified and denied cases in absolute terms.
  • Europe has a relatively high number of applications and a higher proportion of certified cases compared to denied cases.
  • North America has a significant number of applications, with a notable proportion of both certified and denied cases.
  • South America and Africa have fewer applications compared to Asia, Europe, and North America, with varying proportions of certified and denied cases.
  • Oceania has the lowest number of applications.

Does having prior work experience influence the chances of visa certification for career opportunities abroad?¶

In [ ]:
stacked_barplot(data, "has_job_experience", "case_status")

case_status         Certified  Denied    All
has_job_experience                          
All                     17018    8462  25480
N                        5994    4684  10678
Y                       11024    3778  14802
------------------------------------------------------------------------------------------------------------------------
Observations:¶
  • A higher proportion of applicants with job experience (Y) have their cases certified compared to those without job experience (N). Specifically, a larger percentage of 'Y' cases are Certified than 'N' cases.
  • Conversely, a higher proportion of applicants without job experience (N) have their cases denied compared to those with job experience (Y). The percentage of 'N' cases that are Denied is higher than the percentage of 'Y' cases that are Denied.

This suggests that having prior job experience is a positive factor and increases the likelihood of visa certification.

Is the prevailing wage consistent across all regions of the US?¶

In [ ]:
distribution_plot_wrt_target(data, "region_of_employment", "prevailing_wage")
Observations:¶

Based on the distribution plots and boxplots, we can observe the following about the relationship between the region of employment and the prevailing wage:

  • The distribution of prevailing wages appears to vary across different regions of employment. Some regions may have generally higher or lower prevailing wages compared to others.
  • The boxplots indicate that there are differences in the median prevailing wage and the spread of wages across the regions. Some regions might have a wider range of prevailing wages, while others have a more concentrated range.
  • There appear to be outliers in prevailing wages in some regions, which could represent exceptionally high or low wages for certain positions within those regions.

Further investigation would be needed to determine if these differences in prevailing wages across regions significantly impact visa certification outcomes.

Does visa status vary with changes in the prevailing wage set to protect both local talent and foreign workers?¶

In [ ]:
distribution_plot_wrt_target(data, "prevailing_wage", "case_status")
Observations:¶

Based on the distribution plots and boxplots, we can observe the following about the relationship between the prevailing wage and the case status:

  • The distribution of prevailing wages for both Certified and Denied cases seems to be skewed to the right, with a large concentration of cases at lower prevailing wages and fewer cases at higher wages.
  • There appears to be an overlap in the distribution of prevailing wages for both Certified and Denied cases, indicating that prevailing wage alone may not be a definitive factor in determining the case status.
  • However, the boxplots suggest that the median prevailing wage for Certified cases might be slightly higher than for Denied cases.
  • There are outliers with very high prevailing wages in both categories, which could be worth investigating further.

Overall, while there might be a slight tendency for Certified cases to have higher prevailing wages, the overlap in distributions suggests that other factors play a significant role in the visa certification decision.

Does the unit of prevailing wage (Hourly, Weekly, etc.) have any impact on the likelihood of visa application certification?¶

In [ ]:
distribution_plot_wrt_target(data, "unit_of_wage", "case_status")
Observations:¶

Based on the distribution plots, we can observe the following about the relationship between the unit of wage and the case status:

  • The majority of applications use 'Year' as the unit of wage.
  • 'Hour', 'Week', and 'Month' have significantly fewer applications.
  • The distribution of case status (Certified vs. Denied) appears to be different across the units of wage. For example, 'Hourly' wages seem to have a higher proportion of Denied cases compared to 'Year'.

This suggests that the unit of wage might be an important factor influencing the case status. Further investigation into the reasons for these differences would be beneficial.

Data Pre-processing¶

Outlier Check¶

In [ ]:
# outlier detection in numerical columns
numeric_columns = data.select_dtypes(include=np.number).columns.tolist()


plt.figure(figsize=(15, 12))

for i, variable in enumerate(numeric_columns):
    plt.subplot(4, 4, i + 1)
    plt.boxplot(data[variable], whis=1.5)
    plt.tight_layout()
    plt.title(variable)

plt.show()

Outlier Detection Observations¶

Based on the boxplots above:

  • no_of_employees: There are many outliers on the higher end, indicating that a few companies have a significantly larger number of employees compared to the rest.
  • yr_of_estab: There are outliers on the lower end, indicating some companies were established much earlier than the majority.
  • prevailing_wage: There are many outliers on the higher end, indicating some positions have significantly higher prevailing wages than others.
  • Removing outliers from the dataset will result in significant reduction of data, hence we decide not to remove outlier and proceed with them

Data Preparation for modeling¶

Null / Missing Value Treatment¶

In [ ]:
data.isnull().sum()

Observations:¶
  1. There are no nulls or missing values in the dataset.
  2. This dataset also doesn't have any other unique values for missing data
  3. We do not need to perform any additional imputation or treatment for missing values on this dataset

Data Pre-processing¶

In [ ]:
data["case_status"] = data["case_status"].apply(lambda x: 1 if x == "Certified" else 0)

X = data.drop(["case_status", "case_id"],axis=1)
y = data["case_status"]


X = pd.get_dummies(X,drop_first=True)

X_train, X_val, y_train, y_val = train_test_split(
    X, y, test_size=0.3, random_state=1, stratify=y
)

X_val,X_test,y_val,y_test = train_test_split(
    X_val,y_val,test_size=0.1,random_state=1,stratify=y_val
)
Observations:¶
  • We are removing the case_id feature as it has no significance in the dataset.
  • We are also performing sinmple label encoding for case_status feature to make it a numerical column
  • All other categorical colums are transformed using one-hot encoding
In [ ]:
print("Shape of Training set : ", X_train.shape)
print("Shape of Validation set : ", X_val.shape)
print("Shape of test set : ", X_test.shape)
print("Percentage of classes in training set:")
print(y_train.value_counts(normalize=True))
print("Percentage of classes in validation set:")
print(y_val.value_counts(normalize=True))
print("Percentage of classes in test set:")
print(y_test.value_counts(normalize=True))

Shape of Training set :  (17836, 21)
Shape of Validation set :  (6879, 21)
Shape of test set :  (765, 21)
Percentage of classes in training set:
case_status
1    0.667919
0    0.332081
Name: proportion, dtype: float64
Percentage of classes in validation set:
case_status
1    0.66783
0    0.33217
Name: proportion, dtype: float64
Percentage of classes in test set:
case_status
1    0.667974
0    0.332026
Name: proportion, dtype: float64
Observations:¶

After splitting the data:

  • The training set has 17836 rows and 21 columns.
  • The validation set has 6879 rows and 21 columns.
  • The test set has 765 rows and 21 columns.
  • The class distribution (percentage of certified and denied cases) is consistent across the training, validation, and test sets due to the use of stratify=y during the split. This ensures that each set is representative of the original dataset's class distribution.

Model Building¶

Model Evaluation Criterion¶

The model should be good at identifying both certified and denied cases.

Given that misclassifying a denied case as certified could lead to issues for the applicant, and misclassifying a certified case as denied could result in a loss of potential talent, we should consider a metric that balances both precision and recall.

Precision is the ability of the classifier not to label as positive a negative sample, and Recall is the ability of the classifier to find all the positive samples. In this case, a false positive would be predicting a denied case as certified, and a false negative would be predicting a certified case as denied.

The F1-score is a harmonic mean of precision and recall, providing a single score that balances both. Therefore, F1-score would be a suitable metric to evaluate the model's performance, as it considers both false positives and false negatives.

First, let's create functions to calculate different metrics and confusion matrix so that we don't have to use the same code repeatedly for each model.

  • The model_performance_classification_sklearn function will be used to check the model performance of models.
  • The confusion_matrix_sklearn function will be used to plot the confusion matrix.
In [ ]:
# defining a function to compute different metrics to check performance of a classification model built using sklearn


def model_performance_classification_sklearn(model, predictors, target):
    """
    Function to compute different metrics to check classification model performance

    model: classifier
    predictors: independent variables
    target: dependent variable
    """

    # predicting using the independent variables
    pred = model.predict(predictors)

    acc = accuracy_score(target, pred)  # to compute Accuracy
    recall = recall_score(target, pred)  # to compute Recall
    precision = precision_score(target, pred)  # to compute Precision
    f1 = f1_score(target, pred)  # to compute F1-score

    # creating a dataframe of metrics
    df_perf = pd.DataFrame(
        {"Accuracy": acc, "Recall": recall, "Precision": precision, "F1": f1,},
        index=[0],
    )

    return df_perf
In [ ]:
def confusion_matrix_sklearn(model, predictors, target):
    """
    To plot the confusion_matrix with percentages

    model: classifier
    predictors: independent variables
    target: dependent variable
    """
    y_pred = model.predict(predictors)
    cm = confusion_matrix(target, y_pred)
    labels = np.asarray(
        [
            ["{0:0.0f}".format(item) + "\n{0:.2%}".format(item / cm.flatten().sum())]
            for item in cm.flatten()
        ]
    ).reshape(2, 2)

    plt.figure(figsize=(6, 4))
    sns.heatmap(cm, annot=labels, fmt="")
    plt.ylabel("True label")
    plt.xlabel("Predicted label")

Defining scorer to be used for cross-validation and hyperparameter tuning¶

In [ ]:
scorer = metrics.make_scorer(metrics.f1_score)

We are now done with pre-processing and evaluation criterion, so let's start building the model.

Model building with Original data¶

In [ ]:
models = []  # Empty list to store all the models

# Appending models into the list
models.append(("Bagging", BaggingClassifier(random_state=1)))
models.append(("Random forest", RandomForestClassifier(random_state=1)))
models.append(("GBM", GradientBoostingClassifier(random_state=1)))
models.append(("Adaboost", AdaBoostClassifier(random_state=1)))
models.append(("Xgboost", XGBClassifier(random_state=1, eval_metric="logloss")))
models.append(("dtree", DecisionTreeClassifier(random_state=1)))

results_original_data = []  # Empty list to store all model's CV scores
names = []  # Empty list to store name of the models


# loop through all models to get the mean cross validated score
print("\n" "Cross-Validation performance on training dataset:" "\n")

for name, model in models:
    kfold = StratifiedKFold(
        n_splits=5, shuffle=True, random_state=1
    )  # Complete the code to set the number of splits.
    cv_result = cross_val_score(
        estimator=model, X=X_train, y=y_train, scoring = scorer,cv=kfold
    )
    results_original_data.append(cv_result)
    names.append(name)
    print("{}: {}".format(name, cv_result.mean()))

print("\n" "Validation Performance:" "\n")

for name, model in models:
    model.fit(X_train, y_train)
    scores = f1_score(y_val, model.predict(X_val))

    print("{}: {}".format(name, scores))

Cross-Validation performance on training dataset:

Bagging: 0.7756586246579394
Random forest: 0.8037837241749051
GBM: 0.823039791269532
Adaboost: 0.8203377989495703
Xgboost: 0.8095211182586954
dtree: 0.7410652876513099

Validation Performance:

Bagging: 0.7675817565350541
Random forest: 0.7972364702187794
GBM: 0.8195818459969403
Adaboost: 0.8158053488839735
Xgboost: 0.8083918974782968
dtree: 0.7477497255762898
Observations:¶

Based on the cross-validation and validation performance results on the original data:

  • Gradient Boosting (GBM) and AdaBoost perform the best among the tested models in terms of F1-score on both cross-validation and validation sets.
  • Random Forest also shows good performance, but slightly lower than GBM and AdaBoost.
  • Bagging Classifier's performance is decent, but not as good as the boosting models.
  • Decision Tree has the lowest performance, which is expected as it is a simple model and prone to overfitting.
  • The cross-validation scores are generally consistent with the validation scores, indicating that the models are not severely overfitting on the training data.

Given the objective of balancing precision and recall, GBM and AdaBoost appear to be the most promising models to further tune.

In [ ]:
# Plotting boxplots for CV scores of all models defined above
fig = plt.figure(figsize=(10, 7))

fig.suptitle("Algorithm Comparison")
ax = fig.add_subplot(111)

plt.boxplot(results_original_data)
ax.set_xticklabels(names)

plt.show()

Model Building with Oversampled data¶

In [ ]:
print("Before OverSampling, counts of label '1': {}".format(sum(y_train == 1)))
print("Before OverSampling, counts of label '0': {} \n".format(sum(y_train == 0)))

# Synthetic Minority Over Sampling Technique
sm = SMOTE(sampling_strategy=1, k_neighbors=3, random_state=1) ## Complete the code to set the k-nearest neighbors
X_train_over, y_train_over = sm.fit_resample(X_train, y_train)


print("After OverSampling, counts of label '1': {}".format(sum(y_train_over == 1)))
print("After OverSampling, counts of label '0': {} \n".format(sum(y_train_over == 0)))


print("After OverSampling, the shape of train_X: {}".format(X_train_over.shape))
print("After OverSampling, the shape of train_y: {} \n".format(y_train_over.shape))

Before OverSampling, counts of label '1': 11913
Before OverSampling, counts of label '0': 5923 

After OverSampling, counts of label '1': 11913
After OverSampling, counts of label '0': 11913 

After OverSampling, the shape of train_X: (23826, 21)
After OverSampling, the shape of train_y: (23826,)
Observations:¶
  • Before oversampling, the training data was imbalanced with significantly more cases labeled as '1' (Certified) than '0' (Denied).
  • After applying SMOTE with a sampling strategy of 1 and k-neighbors of 3, the number of instances for both classes ('0' and '1') in the training set is now equal (11913 each).
  • The shape of the training data has increased from (17836, 21) to (23826, 21) due to the generation of synthetic samples for the minority class.

This balanced dataset will now be used to train the models to see if oversampling improves performance, particularly in identifying the minority class.

In [ ]:
models = []

models.append(("Bagging", BaggingClassifier(random_state=1)))
models.append(("Random forest", RandomForestClassifier(random_state=1)))
models.append(("GBM", GradientBoostingClassifier(random_state=1)))
models.append(("Adaboost", AdaBoostClassifier(random_state=1)))
models.append(("Xgboost", XGBClassifier(random_state=1, eval_metric="logloss")))
models.append(("dtree", DecisionTreeClassifier(random_state=1)))

results_oversampled_data = []
names = []

print("\n" "Cross-Validation performance on training dataset:" "\n")

for name, model in models:
    kfold = StratifiedKFold(
        n_splits=5, shuffle=True, random_state=1
    )
    cv_result = cross_val_score(
        estimator=model, X=X_train_over, y=y_train_over,scoring = scorer, cv=kfold
    )
    results_oversampled_data.append(cv_result)
    names.append(name)
    print("{}: {}".format(name, cv_result.mean()))

print("\n" "Validation Performance:" "\n")

for name, model in models:
    model.fit(X_train_over,y_train_over)
    scores = f1_score(y_val, model.predict(X_val))

    print("{}: {}".format(name, scores))

Cross-Validation performance on training dataset:

Bagging: 0.7580337421338358
Random forest: 0.7951775417351203
GBM: 0.8069967802760851
Adaboost: 0.7947705548493091
Xgboost: 0.8006374982852782
dtree: 0.7250754818656848

Validation Performance:

Bagging: 0.76242115746376
Random forest: 0.792416860834569
GBM: 0.8104452767374116
Adaboost: 0.8098009188361409
Xgboost: 0.8023740108288213
dtree: 0.746268656716418
Observations:¶

Based on the cross-validation and validation performance results on the oversampled data:

  • The performance of the models on the oversampled data shows a slight reduction when compared to the original data.
  • Gradient Boosting (GBM) and AdaBoost still perform well, but their relative performance compared to other models might have shifted slightly.
  • The F1-scores on the validation set provide a good indication of how well the models generalize to unseen, imbalanced data after being trained on balanced data.
  • Comparing these results with the performance on the original data and undersampled data will help determine the best approach for handling the class imbalance for this problem.
In [ ]:
fig = plt.figure(figsize=(10, 7))

fig.suptitle("Algorithm Comparison")
ax = fig.add_subplot(111)

plt.boxplot(results_oversampled_data)
ax.set_xticklabels(names)

plt.show()

Model Building with Undersampled data¶

In [ ]:
under_sampling = RandomUnderSampler(random_state=1, sampling_strategy=1)
X_train_under, y_train_under = under_sampling.fit_resample(X_train, y_train)


print("Before UnderSampling, counts of label '1': {}".format(sum(y_train == 1)))
print("Before UnderSampling, counts of label '0': {} \n".format(sum(y_train == 0)))


print("After UnderSampling, counts of label '1': {}".format(sum(y_train_under == 1)))
print("After UnderSampling, counts of label '0': {} \n".format(sum(y_train_under == 0)))


print("After UnderSampling, the shape of train_X: {}".format(X_train_under.shape))
print("After UnderSampling, the shape of train_y: {} \n".format(X_train_under.shape))

Before UnderSampling, counts of label '1': 11913
Before UnderSampling, counts of label '0': 5923 

After UnderSampling, counts of label '1': 5923
After UnderSampling, counts of label '0': 5923 

After UnderSampling, the shape of train_X: (11846, 21)
After UnderSampling, the shape of train_y: (11846, 21)
Observations:¶
  • Before undersampling, the training data was imbalanced with significantly more cases labeled as '1' (Certified) than '0' (Denied).
  • After applying Random Under Sampling with a sampling strategy of 1, the number of instances for both classes ('0' and '1') in the training set is now equal (5923 each).
  • The shape of the training data has decreased from (17836, 21) to (11846, 21) due to the removal of instances from the majority class.

This balanced dataset will now be used to train the models to see if undersampling improves performance, particularly in identifying the minority class.

In [ ]:
models = []

models.append(("Bagging", BaggingClassifier(random_state=1)))
models.append(("Random forest", RandomForestClassifier(random_state=1)))
models.append(("GBM", GradientBoostingClassifier(random_state=1)))
models.append(("Adaboost", AdaBoostClassifier(random_state=1)))
models.append(("Xgboost", XGBClassifier(random_state=1, eval_metric="logloss")))
models.append(("dtree", DecisionTreeClassifier(random_state=1)))

results_undersampled_data = []
names = []

print("\n" "Cross-Validation performance on training dataset:" "\n")

for name, model in models:
    kfold = StratifiedKFold(
        n_splits=5, shuffle=True, random_state=1
    )
    cv_result = cross_val_score(
        estimator=model, X=X_train_under, y=y_train_under,scoring = scorer, cv=kfold,n_jobs =-1
    )
    results_undersampled_data.append(cv_result)
    names.append(name)
    print("{}: {}".format(name, cv_result.mean()))

print("\n" "Validation Performance:" "\n")

for name, model in models:
    model.fit(X_train_under,y_train_under)
    scores = f1_score(y_val, model.predict(X_val))

    print("{}: {}".format(name, scores))

Cross-Validation performance on training dataset:

Bagging: 0.6411413525524321
Random forest: 0.6875011408129813
GBM: 0.7131358906535971
Adaboost: 0.6949405744215158
Xgboost: 0.6944693136408734
dtree: 0.617022679979161

Validation Performance:

Bagging: 0.6916956737941323
Random forest: 0.734144015259895
GBM: 0.7608695652173914
Adaboost: 0.7604202747950584
Xgboost: 0.7423652871123688
dtree: 0.6839080459770115
Observations:¶

Based on the cross-validation and validation performance results on the undersampled data:

  • The performance of the models on the undersampled data is generally lower compared to the original and oversampled data.
  • Gradient Boosting (GBM) and AdaBoost still show relatively better performance among the tested models, but their F1-scores are lower.
  • Undersampling significantly reduces the size of the training data, which can lead to a loss of potentially useful information and result in lower model performance.

Given the lower performance on the undersampled data, it seems that this approach might not be the most suitable for this dataset compared to using the original imbalanced data or oversampling.

In [ ]:
fig = plt.figure(figsize=(10, 7))

fig.suptitle("Algorithm Comparison")
ax = fig.add_subplot(111)

plt.boxplot(results_undersampled_data)
ax.set_xticklabels(names)

plt.show()

Hyperparameter Tuning¶

Based on the initial model evaluation on the original data, Gradient Boosting (GBM) and AdaBoost performed the best in terms of F1-score. Therefore, we will proceed with hyperparameter tuning for these two models to further optimize their performance. The models selected for hyperparameter tuning are:

  • AdaBoost on original data
  • GBM on original data
  • GBM on oversampled data

After model tuning, we will compare the f1 scores of each of these tuned models and finalize the best performing model to fit on test data

AdaBoostClassifier Hyperparamter tuning on original data¶

Best practices for hyperparameter tuning in AdaBoost:

n_estimators:

  • Start with a specific number (50 is used in general) and increase in steps: 50, 75, 85, 100

  • Use fewer estimators (e.g., 50 to 100) if using complex base learners (like deeper decision trees)

  • Use more estimators (e.g., 100 to 150) when learning rate is low (e.g., 0.1 or lower)

  • Avoid very high values unless performance keeps improving on validation

learning_rate:

  • Common values to try: 1.0, 0.5, 0.1, 0.01

  • Use 1.0 for faster training, suitable for fewer estimators

  • Use 0.1 or 0.01 when using more estimators to improve generalization

  • Avoid very small values (< 0.01) unless you plan to use many estimators (e.g., >500) and have sufficient data

In [ ]:
%%time
# defining model
Model = AdaBoostClassifier(random_state=1)

# Parameter grid to pass in RandomSearchCV
param_grid = {
    "n_estimators": np.arange(10, 40, 10),
    "learning_rate": [1.0, 0.5, 0.1, 0.01],
    "estimator": [
        DecisionTreeClassifier(max_depth=1, random_state=1),
        DecisionTreeClassifier(max_depth=2, random_state=1),
        DecisionTreeClassifier(max_depth=3, random_state=1),
    ],
}

# Type of scoring used to compare parameter combinations
scorer = metrics.make_scorer(metrics.f1_score)

#Calling RandomizedSearchCV
randomized_cv = RandomizedSearchCV(estimator=Model, param_distributions=param_grid, n_jobs = -1, n_iter=50, scoring=scorer, cv=5, random_state=1)

#Fitting parameters in RandomizedSearchCV
randomized_cv.fit(X_train, y_train)

print("Best parameters are {} with CV score={}:" .format(randomized_cv.best_params_,randomized_cv.best_score_))

Best parameters are {'n_estimators': np.int64(20), 'learning_rate': 0.1, 'estimator': DecisionTreeClassifier(max_depth=3, random_state=1)} with CV score=0.8226755688644527:
CPU times: user 1.32 s, sys: 121 ms, total: 1.44 s
Wall time: 52.9 s

We have found a best estimator. Let's now use this to create a tuned Adaboost model and fit it on our training data

In [ ]:
tuned_adb = randomized_cv.best_estimator_
tuned_adb.fit(X_train, y_train)
Out[ ]:
AdaBoostClassifier(estimator=DecisionTreeClassifier(max_depth=3,
                                                    random_state=1),
                   learning_rate=0.1, n_estimators=np.int64(20),
                   random_state=1)
In a Jupyter environment, please rerun this cell to show the HTML representation or trust the notebook.
On GitHub, the HTML representation is unable to render, please try loading this page with nbviewer.org.
AdaBoostClassifier(estimator=DecisionTreeClassifier(max_depth=3,
                                                    random_state=1),
                   learning_rate=0.1, n_estimators=np.int64(20),
                   random_state=1)
DecisionTreeClassifier(max_depth=3, random_state=1)
DecisionTreeClassifier(max_depth=3, random_state=1)

Let's calculate the performance on training data

In [ ]:
adb_train = model_performance_classification_sklearn(tuned_adb, X_train, y_train)
adb_train
Out[ ]:
Accuracy Recall Precision F1
0 0.73318 0.926719 0.739649 0.822683

Let's calculate the performance on validation data and see if it generalizes well

In [ ]:
adb_val = model_performance_classification_sklearn(tuned_adb, X_val, y_val)
adb_val
Out[ ]:
Accuracy Recall Precision F1
0 0.722489 0.925337 0.730789 0.816636
Observations:¶

Based on the AdaBoostClassifier hyperparameter tuning on the original data:

  • The best parameters found are n_estimators=20, learning_rate=0.1, and estimator=DecisionTreeClassifier(max_depth=3, random_state=1).
  • The model trained with these parameters achieved an F1-score of approximately 0.8227 on the cross-validation sets.
  • The training performance shows a high recall (0.9267) and a decent precision (0.7396), resulting in an F1-score of 0.8227.
  • The validation performance is slightly lower with an F1-score of 0.8166, indicating a small drop in performance on unseen data, but the model still generalizes reasonably well.

GBM Hyperparameter tuning on original data¶

Best practices for hyperparameter tuning in Gradient Boosting:

n_estimators:

  • Start with 100 (default) and increase: 100, 200, 300, 500
  • Typically, higher values lead to better performance, but they also increase training time
  • Use 200–500 for larger datasets or complex problems
  • Monitor validation performance to avoid overfitting, as too many estimators can degrade generalization

learning_rate:

  • Common values to try: 0.1, 0.05, 0.01, 0.005
  • Use lower values (e.g., 0.01 or 0.005) if you are using many estimators (e.g., > 200)
  • Higher learning rates (e.g., 0.1) can be used with fewer estimators for faster convergence
  • Always balance the learning rate with n_estimators to prevent overfitting or underfitting

subsample:

  • Common values: 0.7, 0.8, 0.9, 1.0
  • Use a value between 0.7 and 0.9 for improved generalization by introducing randomness
  • 1.0 uses the full dataset for each boosting round, potentially leading to overfitting
  • Reducing subsample can help reduce overfitting, especially in smaller datasets

max_features:

  • Common values: "sqrt", "log2", or float (e.g., 0.3, 0.5)
  • "sqrt" (default) works well for classification tasks
  • Lower values (e.g., 0.3) help reduce overfitting by limiting the number of features considered at each split
In [ ]:
%%time

#Creating pipeline
Model = GradientBoostingClassifier(random_state=1)

#Parameter grid to pass in RandomSearchCV
param_grid = {
    "init": [AdaBoostClassifier(random_state=1),DecisionTreeClassifier(random_state=1)],
    "n_estimators": np.arange(125,175,25),
    "learning_rate": [0.01, 0.2, 0.05, 1],
    "subsample":[0.8,0.9,1],
    "max_features":[0.5,0.7,1],
}

# Type of scoring used to compare parameter combinations
scorer = metrics.make_scorer(metrics.f1_score)

#Calling RandomizedSearchCV
randomized_cv = RandomizedSearchCV(estimator=Model, param_distributions=param_grid, n_iter=50, scoring=scorer, cv=5, random_state=1, n_jobs = -1)

#Fitting parameters in RandomizedSearchCV
randomized_cv.fit(X_train,y_train)

print("Best parameters are {} with CV score={}:" .format(randomized_cv.best_params_,randomized_cv.best_score_))

Best parameters are {'subsample': 0.8, 'n_estimators': np.int64(150), 'max_features': 0.7, 'learning_rate': 0.05, 'init': AdaBoostClassifier(random_state=1)} with CV score=0.8246237382763848:
CPU times: user 4.41 s, sys: 340 ms, total: 4.75 s
Wall time: 5min 48s

We have found a best estimator. Let's now use this to create a tuned GBM model for original data and fit it on our training data

In [ ]:
tuned_gbm = randomized_cv.best_estimator_
tuned_gbm.fit(X_train, y_train)
Out[ ]:
GradientBoostingClassifier(init=AdaBoostClassifier(random_state=1),
                           learning_rate=0.05, max_features=0.7,
                           n_estimators=np.int64(150), random_state=1,
                           subsample=0.8)
In a Jupyter environment, please rerun this cell to show the HTML representation or trust the notebook.
On GitHub, the HTML representation is unable to render, please try loading this page with nbviewer.org.
GradientBoostingClassifier(init=AdaBoostClassifier(random_state=1),
                           learning_rate=0.05, max_features=0.7,
                           n_estimators=np.int64(150), random_state=1,
                           subsample=0.8)
AdaBoostClassifier(random_state=1)
AdaBoostClassifier(random_state=1)

Let's calculate the performance on training data

In [ ]:
gbm_train = model_performance_classification_sklearn(
    tuned_gbm, X_train, y_train
)
gbm_train
Out[ ]:
Accuracy Recall Precision F1
0 0.756055 0.880467 0.781828 0.828221

Let's calculate the performance on validation data and see if it generalizes well

In [ ]:
gbm_val = model_performance_classification_sklearn(
    tuned_gbm, X_val, y_val
)
gbm_val
Out[ ]:
Accuracy Recall Precision F1
0 0.741678 0.870701 0.771754 0.818247
Observations:¶

Based on the GBM hyperparameter tuning on the original data:

  • The best parameters found are subsample=0.8, n_estimators=150, max_features=0.7, learning_rate=0.05, and init=AdaBoostClassifier(random_state=1).
  • The model trained with these parameters achieved an F1-score of approximately 0.8246 on the cross-validation sets.
  • The training performance shows a good balance of accuracy, recall, precision, and F1-score. The F1-score on training data is 0.8282
  • The validation performance is slightly lower than the training performance, suggesting a small degree of overfitting, but the model still generalizes well with an F1-score of 0.8182.

GBM Hyperparameter tuning on oversampled data¶

Best practices for hyperparameter tuning in Gradient Boosting:

n_estimators:

  • Start with 100 (default) and increase: 100, 200, 300, 500
  • Typically, higher values lead to better performance, but they also increase training time
  • Use 200–500 for larger datasets or complex problems
  • Monitor validation performance to avoid overfitting, as too many estimators can degrade generalization

learning_rate:

  • Common values to try: 0.1, 0.05, 0.01, 0.005
  • Use lower values (e.g., 0.01 or 0.005) if you are using many estimators (e.g., > 200)
  • Higher learning rates (e.g., 0.1) can be used with fewer estimators for faster convergence
  • Always balance the learning rate with n_estimators to prevent overfitting or underfitting

subsample:

  • Common values: 0.7, 0.8, 0.9, 1.0
  • Use a value between 0.7 and 0.9 for improved generalization by introducing randomness
  • 1.0 uses the full dataset for each boosting round, potentially leading to overfitting
  • Reducing subsample can help reduce overfitting, especially in smaller datasets

max_features:

  • Common values: "sqrt", "log2", or float (e.g., 0.3, 0.5)
  • "sqrt" (default) works well for classification tasks
  • Lower values (e.g., 0.3) help reduce overfitting by limiting the number of features considered at each split
In [ ]:
%%time

#Creating pipeline
Model = GradientBoostingClassifier(random_state=1)

#Parameter grid to pass in RandomSearchCV
param_grid = {
    "init": [AdaBoostClassifier(random_state=1),DecisionTreeClassifier(random_state=1)],
    "n_estimators": np.arange(125,175,25),
    "learning_rate": [0.01, 0.2, 0.05, 1],
    "subsample":[0.8,0.9,1],
    "max_features":[0.5,0.7,1],
}

# Type of scoring used to compare parameter combinations
scorer = metrics.make_scorer(metrics.f1_score)

#Calling RandomizedSearchCV
randomized_cv = RandomizedSearchCV(estimator=Model, param_distributions=param_grid, n_iter=50, scoring=scorer, cv=5, random_state=1, n_jobs = -1)

#Fitting parameters in RandomizedSearchCV
randomized_cv.fit(X_train_over,y_train_over)

print("Best parameters are {} with CV score={}:" .format(randomized_cv.best_params_,randomized_cv.best_score_))

Best parameters are {'subsample': 0.9, 'n_estimators': np.int64(125), 'max_features': 0.5, 'learning_rate': 0.2, 'init': AdaBoostClassifier(random_state=1)} with CV score=0.8020782140833143:
CPU times: user 5.63 s, sys: 462 ms, total: 6.09 s
Wall time: 7min 55s

We have found a best estimator. Let's now use this to create a tuned GMB model for oversampled data and fit it on our training data

In [ ]:
tuned_gbm_over = randomized_cv.best_estimator_
tuned_gbm_over.fit(X_train_over, y_train_over)
Out[ ]:
GradientBoostingClassifier(init=AdaBoostClassifier(random_state=1),
                           learning_rate=0.2, max_features=0.5,
                           n_estimators=np.int64(125), random_state=1,
                           subsample=0.9)

GradientBoostingClassifier(init=AdaBoostClassifier(random_state=1),
                           learning_rate=0.2, max_features=0.5,
                           n_estimators=np.int64(125), random_state=1,
                           subsample=0.9)
AdaBoostClassifier(random_state=1)
AdaBoostClassifier(random_state=1)

Let's calculate the performance on training data

In [ ]:
gbm_train_over = model_performance_classification_sklearn(
    tuned_gbm_over, X_train_over, y_train_over
)
gbm_train_over
Out[ ]:
Accuracy Recall Precision F1
0 0.80408 0.862839 0.772102 0.814953

Let's calculate the performance on validation data and see if it generalizes well

In [ ]:
gbm_val_over = model_performance_classification_sklearn(
    tuned_gbm_over, X_val, y_val
)
gbm_val_over
Out[ ]:
Accuracy Recall Precision F1
0 0.739788 0.850675 0.779729 0.813658
Observations:¶

Based on the GBM hyperparameter tuning on oversampled data:

  • The best parameters found are subsample=0.9, n_estimators=125, max_features=0.5, learning_rate=0.2, and init=AdaBoostClassifier(random_state=1).
  • The model trained with these parameters achieved an F1-score of approximately 0.8021 on the cross-validation sets.
  • The training performance shows a good balance of accuracy, recall, precision, and F1-score. The F1-score on training data is 0.8150
  • The validation performance is slightly lower than the training performance, suggesting a small degree of overfitting, but the model still generalizes well with an F1-score of 0.8137.

Model Performance Summary and Final Model Selection¶

In [264]:
# training performance comparison

models_train_comp_df = pd.concat(
    [
        gbm_train.T,
        gbm_train_over.T,
        adb_train.T,
    ],
    axis=1,
)
models_train_comp_df.columns = [
    "Gradient boosting trained with Original data",
    "Gradient boosting trained with Oversampled data",
    "AdaBoost trained with Original data",
]
print("Training performance comparison:")
models_train_comp_df

Training performance comparison:
Out[264]:
Gradient boosting trained with Original data Gradient boosting trained with Oversampled data AdaBoost trained with Original data
Accuracy 0.756055 0.804080 0.733180
Recall 0.880467 0.862839 0.926719
Precision 0.781828 0.772102 0.739649
F1 0.828221 0.814953 0.822683
Observations:¶

Based on the training performance comparison of the tuned models:

  • The Gradient Boosting model trained on the original data has the highest F1-score (0.8282) and a good balance of precision (0.7818) and recall (0.8805).
  • The AdaBoost model trained on the original data has a slightly lower F1-score (0.8227) but a very high recall (0.9267), indicating it's very good at identifying positive cases. Its precision (0.7396) is lower than the GBM model.
  • The Gradient Boosting model trained on oversampled data has a lower F1-score (0.8150) compared to the models trained on original data. Its recall (0.8628) is lower than both models trained on original data, but its precision (0.7721) is comparable to the GBM model trained on original data.

Based on training performance alone, the Gradient Boosting model trained on original data seems to perform best in terms of F1-score, while the AdaBoost model has the highest recall. The oversampled data does not appear to improve training performance significantly in terms of F1-score.

In [265]:
# Validation performance comparison

models_train_comp_df = pd.concat(
    [ gbm_val.T, gbm_val_over.T, adb_val.T], axis=1,
)
models_train_comp_df.columns = [
    "Gradient boosting trained with Original data",
    "Gradient boosting trained with Oversampled data",
    "AdaBoost trained with Original data",
]
print("Validation performance comparison:")
models_train_comp_df

Validation performance comparison:
Out[265]:
Gradient boosting trained with Original data Gradient boosting trained with Oversampled data AdaBoost trained with Original data
Accuracy 0.741678 0.739788 0.722489
Recall 0.870701 0.850675 0.925337
Precision 0.771754 0.779729 0.730789
F1 0.818247 0.813658 0.816636
Observations:¶

Based on the validation performance comparison of the tuned models:

  • The Gradient Boosting model trained on the original data has the highest F1-score (0.8182) among the models, indicating good generalization to unseen data. It also shows a reasonable balance of precision (0.7718) and recall (0.8707).
  • The Gradient Boosting model trained on oversampled data has a slightly lower F1-score (0.8137) than the one trained on original data. While its precision (0.7797) is slightly higher, its recall (0.8507) is lower.
  • The AdaBoost model trained on the original data has an F1-score (0.8166) close to the GBM models. It stands out with the highest recall (0.9253), meaning it is very effective at identifying positive cases, but its precision (0.7308) is the lowest among the three.

Considering the F1-score as the primary evaluation metric, the Gradient Boosting model trained on the original data performs slightly better on the validation set. However, the AdaBoost model's high recall might be beneficial depending on the specific business needs and the cost of false negatives.

In [266]:
ada_test = model_performance_classification_sklearn(tuned_adb, X_test, y_test)
ada_test
Out[266]:
Accuracy Recall Precision F1
0 0.741176 0.933464 0.74415 0.828125
In [267]:
gbm_test = model_performance_classification_sklearn(tuned_gbm, X_test, y_test)
gbm_test
Out[267]:
Accuracy Recall Precision F1
0 0.752941 0.888454 0.774744 0.827712
Observations:¶

Based on the performance of the tuned models on the test set:

  • The tuned AdaBoost model (tuned_adb) achieved an F1-score of 0.8281. It has a high recall of 0.9335, meaning it is very good at identifying certified cases, and a precision of 0.7441.
  • The tuned Gradient Boosting model (tuned_gbm) achieved an F1-score of 0.8277. It has a recall of 0.8885 and a precision of 0.7747.

Both models perform very similarly on the test set in terms of F1-score. The AdaBoost model has slightly higher recall, while the Gradient Boosting model has slightly higher precision. The choice between these two models might depend on whether prioritizing the identification of certified cases (recall) or minimizing false positives (precision) is more important for the business problem.

In [268]:
feature_names = X_train.columns
importances = tuned_adb.feature_importances_
indices = np.argsort(importances)

plt.figure(figsize=(8, 5))
plt.title("Feature Importances")
plt.barh(range(len(indices)), importances[indices], color="violet", align="center")
plt.yticks(range(len(indices)), [feature_names[i] for i in indices])
plt.xlabel("Relative Importance")
plt.show()
Observations:¶

Based on the feature importance plot for the tuned AdaBoost model:

  • has_job_experince_Y is the most important feature, significantly influencing the model's predictions.
  • Other important features include prevailing_wage, unit_of_wage_Year continent_Europe, and the dummy variables created for education_of_employee and region_of_employment.
  • Most of the other features have very low feature importances, suggesting they are less influential in this model.
In [269]:
feature_names = X_train.columns
importances = tuned_gbm.feature_importances_
indices = np.argsort(importances)

plt.figure(figsize=(8, 5))
plt.title("Feature Importances")
plt.barh(range(len(indices)), importances[indices], color="violet", align="center")
plt.yticks(range(len(indices)), [feature_names[i] for i in indices])
plt.xlabel("Relative Importance")
plt.show()
Observations:¶

Based on the feature importance plot for the tuned Gradient Boosting model:

  • prevailing_wage and education_of_employee_High_School are the most important feature, significantly influencing the model's predictions.
  • Other important features include has_job_experience_Y, no_of_employees, unit_of_wage_Year and the dummy variables created for education_of_employee, region_of_employment, and continent.
  • GBM model has a lot more important features than the AdaBoost one

These feature importances highlight the key factors that the Gradient Boosting model considers most influential in determining visa certification.

Actionable Insights and Recommendations¶

Based on the exploratory data analysis and the performance of the tuned models, particularly the Gradient Boosting and AdaBoost models, the following actionable insights and recommendations can be made to facilitate the visa approval process and recommend suitable applicant profiles:

Key Factors Influencing Visa Certification:

  • Prevailing Wage: The analysis clearly shows that prevailing wage is a significant predictor of visa certification. Ensuring that the offered wage meets or exceeds the prevailing wage for the specific occupation and location is crucial for a successful application.
  • Education Level: Higher education levels, especially Master's and Doctorate degrees, are strongly associated with a higher likelihood of visa certification. Encouraging applicants with higher education or those willing to pursue further education could improve approval rates.
  • Job Experience: Having prior job experience positively impacts the chances of visa certification. Applicants with relevant work experience should highlight it in their applications.
  • Region of Employment: While not as strongly influential as prevailing wage or education, the region of employment does show some variation in prevailing wages and potentially in certification rates. Further analysis into region-specific requirements or trends might be beneficial.

Recommendations for EasyVisa and OFLC:

  • Develop a Predictive Tool: Utilize the tuned Gradient Boosting or AdaBoost model to create a tool that can predict the likelihood of visa certification based on applicant and employer data. This tool can help in pre-screening applications and prioritizing those with a higher chance of approval.
  • Provide Guidance to Applicants: Based on the identified key factors, EasyVisa can provide targeted guidance to applicants on how to strengthen their profiles. This includes advising on education, gaining relevant work experience, and ensuring the offered wage is competitive.
  • Streamline Processing: For applications with a high predicted likelihood of certification based on the model, OFLC could potentially streamline the review process, allowing for faster approvals.
  • Focus on High-Risk Applications: The model can also help identify applications with a higher risk of denial. These cases can be flagged for more thorough review by OFLC.
  • Continuous Monitoring and Model Updates: The visa landscape and job market can change over time. It is recommended to continuously monitor the model's performance and retrain it with updated data periodically to ensure its accuracy and relevance.

By leveraging the insights from this analysis and implementing a predictive model, EasyVisa and OFLC can enhance the efficiency and effectiveness of the visa application process, benefiting both employers and foreign workers while upholding the integrity of the immigration system.

Power Ahead
